1: ʾĕlōhîm bĕshimĕkhā hôshîʿênî — O God, save me by your name. Bĕshimĕkhā (by your name — the divine name as a power-source for salvation; the name concentrates the identity and authority of the one named). ûbigĕbûrātĕkhā tĕdînnênî — vindicate me by your strength. Gĕbûrāh (strength, might — the military power of YHWH). Dîn (to judge, vindicate — to render a verdict in favor of the one pleading). The petition: salvation by divine name + vindication by divine strength = the complete rescue the Psalmist needs.
2: ʾĕlōhîm shĕmaʿ tĕphillātî — hear my prayer, O God. Shāmaʿ (to hear, listen attentively — the first action required of the divine judge). haʾăzînāh lĕʾimrê-phî — listen to what I am saying. ʾĀzan (hiphil: to give ear — the attentive cupping of the ear to receive the petition). The double petition for divine attention before the situation is described.
3: kî zārîm qāmû ʿālay — for outsiders are threatening me. Zārîm (strangers, foreigners, outsiders — those who do not belong to the covenant community; from zûr: to be a stranger). In the historical context the Ziphites were Judahites who betrayed David to Saul (1 Sam 23:19; 26:1) — they became functionally zārîm (strangers) by their betrayal, crossing from the covenant community to the enemy's side. wĕʿārîṣîm biqqĕshû nafshî — violent men are hunting my life. ʿĀrîṣ (violent, ruthless — one who uses force without restraint). lōʾ śāmû ʾĕlōhîm lĕnegdām — they have no thought of God. Śîm ʾĕlōhîm lĕneged (to set God before one — the interior orientation of the heart toward God; cf. Ps 16:8: shiwwîtî YHWH lĕnegdî tāmîd: I have set YHWH always before me). The betrayers do not have God in view — practical atheism as the root of betrayal, mirroring Ps 53:1.
4: hinnēh ʾĕlōhîm ʿōzēr lî — but God is my helper. Hinnēh (behold — the particle of dramatic attention) + ʿōzēr (helper, one who gives aid — from ʿāzar: to help). The confident counter to the threat: God as active helper. ʾădōnāy bĕsōmĕkhê nafshî — the Lord is the one who sustains my life. Sāmakh (to support, sustain — the upholding of the life/soul). Nafshî (my soul/life — the full personal existence sustained by divine support).
5: yāshîb hārāʿ lĕshōrĕrāy — he will bring the evil back on my enemies. Shûb ʿāl (hiphil: to make return upon — the divine return of the evil the enemies intended to the senders; the boomerang-justice of divine retribution). baʾămittĕkhā haṣmîtēm — in your faithfulness, silence them. Ṣāmat (to cut off, silence — the silencing of those who acted against the trusting one). ʾĔmûnāh (faithfulness) as the ground of the silencing — YHWH's faithfulness to his covenant is the basis for the petition.
6: binĕdābāh ʾezbĕḥāh lākh — with a freewill offering I will sacrifice to you. Nĕdābāh (freewill offering — the voluntary sacrifice beyond what is required by law; offered purely from gratitude, not from obligation; from nādab: to volunteer). The highest form of sacrifice — the one that cannot be commanded because it must arise spontaneously from a grateful heart. ʾôdeh shimĕkhā YHWH kî-ṭôb — I will give thanks to your name, LORD, for it is good. The thanksgiving (tôdāh-type praise) to the divine name — echoing the theme of Ps 52:9 and Ps 50:14.
7: kî mikkol-ṣārāh hiṣṣîlānî — for he has rescued me from every distress. The past-tense certainty: though the situation is still dangerous (the Ziphites are still a threat), the prayer expresses the confidence of completed rescue. The perfect tense (hiṣṣîlānî: he has rescued me) anticipates the divine action as already accomplished — the grammar of faith. ûbĕʾōybay rāʾătāh ʿênî — and my eye has seen the downfall of my enemies. Rāʾāh ʿênî (my eye has seen) — the testimony of personal witness to the divine vindication. The same confident vision as Ps 52:6 (the righteous see the wicked's fate) and Ps 54:7 closes the psalm with that same perspective: the trusting person's eye sees what the enemy plotted against them rebound on the enemy.
